title: One Workflow Engine to Rule Them All
context: The platform team has proposed standardising the entire organisation on a single workflow orchestration platform — they're pitching Azure Logic Apps as the one tool for every process: HR onboarding flows, payroll run orchestration, absence approvals, integration pipelines to third-party systems, and long-running compliance case management. The Platform Architect is presenting to a cross-team architecture review. He has strong buy-in from the Head of Operations who is tired of every team using different tools. You are the solution architect for the HR domain. The CTO is chairing.

questions.prompt: The Platform Architect opens: "Right now we have four different orchestration tools across the platform: Logic Apps, Durable Functions, Power Automate, and some teams rolling their own with queues and timers. It's a support nightmare. I'm proposing we standardise everything on Logic Apps. One tool, one operational model, one team responsible for governance." The Head of Operations adds: "Finally. I've been asking for this for two years." How should you respond?

options.text: Standardisation makes sense from both an operational and a skills perspective. Logic Apps is well-supported on Azure, integrates natively with the services we already use, and the platform team has deep expertise in it. Maintaining four different orchestration tools means four different monitoring approaches, four different deployment pipelines, and four different on-call runbooks. That's unsustainable. I'd support migrating all our workflows to Logic Apps as part of the next planning cycle — we can prioritise by domain, start with the integration-heavy ones where Logic Apps is already the strongest fit, and work through the more complex orchestrations once the team has established the patterns and guardrails.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. You've agreed to a 'one engine for everything' mandate without testing whether Logic Apps is actually the right fit for all four workflow categories in play. Logic Apps is an excellent connector and integration platform, but it is a poor host for code-heavy domain logic, long-running stateful processes with complex retry semantics, or workflows where business logic belongs in the owning service. Agreeing upfront means your payroll run orchestration — which has complex transactional requirements — will end up in a visual designer it was never designed for.

options.text: I want to support standardisation, but I think we're conflating four different classes of problem and assuming one tool serves all of them equally well. Let me name them: connector workflows — calling third-party APIs, syncing data, integrating with external systems — Logic Apps is genuinely the right fit. Human approval flows — absence requests, expense sign-offs — Power Automate or Logic Apps with SharePoint works well. Code-owned domain orchestration — payroll run sequencing with compensating transactions, retry semantics, and exact-once guarantees — that's Durable Functions territory. Case management — long-running compliance investigations — that's a different class again. My concern isn't standardisation; it's forcing domain process logic into a visual designer that can't own it properly.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. This is the exact right response for the 'one workflow engine' objection. You've supported the intent (cut tool sprawl, improve governance) while exposing the architectural flaw: not all processes are the same class of problem. Logic Apps is a superb integration and connector orchestrator. It is a poor host for code-heavy domain workflows with complex compensating transaction logic. By naming the four categories, you've given the room a taxonomy to reason with rather than a simple yes or no.

options.text: Standardising on Logic Apps creates a significant vendor lock-in risk that we should evaluate carefully. If Microsoft materially changes the pricing model, deprecates key connectors, or shifts strategic direction toward a different orchestration product, we'd have our entire workflow estate locked to a platform we can't easily migrate away from. We've already seen this with other Azure services where pricing changes came without much warning. I'd recommend we at least evaluate a more portable orchestration framework — something like Temporal or Apache Airflow — that gives us the standardisation benefits without tying every workflow to a single vendor's proprietary platform.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. Vendor lock-in is a real but secondary concern here, and it's not the most useful thing to raise. You're already deeply on Azure — Logic Apps lock-in is a minor incremental risk relative to the platform dependency you already have. Leading with this concern makes you look like you're looking for a reason to block rather than engaging with the real problem, which is architectural fit for different process categories.

questions.prompt: The Platform Architect pushes back: "I've heard the 'different tools for different problems' argument before, but in practice it just means every team does whatever they want and nobody can support it. One operational model with clear guardrails is better than perfect architectural purity." The Head of Operations nods vigorously. The CTO looks sceptical of your position. How should you respond?

options.text: You're right operationally, and I don't want to be the person defending tool sprawl. Let me be more specific about where I'm drawing the line. I'm fine with Logic Apps as the default for integration, connectors, and approval flows — that covers probably 70% of what we're being asked to standardise. What I'm asking for is a carve-out for domain-owned long-running orchestration in payroll specifically, where the correctness guarantees matter more than a unified operations model. Payroll run processing has exact-once requirements and compensating transaction logic that Durable Functions handles and Logic Apps doesn't. That's one carve-out, not a rejection of the whole proposal.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. This is how you handle pushback while defending a technically valid position. You've acknowledged the operational concern, moved off the abstract 'different tools' argument to a specific and justified exception, and quantified the concession (70% standardised on Logic Apps is a huge win for the platform team). The payroll carve-out is defensible because exact-once guarantee is a real, concrete requirement that Logic Apps cannot deliver. You've given the CTO something to agree to rather than a standoff.

options.text: I agree we should standardise the operational model and governance, even if the implementation tool varies by workflow category. We could establish Logic Apps as the organisational standard for monitoring, alerting, naming conventions, and deployment processes, while allowing teams to use Durable Functions where the domain complexity genuinely demands it — provided they adopt the same operational standards. That way the support team gets a consistent operational model regardless of the underlying engine, and domain teams retain the flexibility to choose the right tool for their specific problem. The governance is unified even if the technology isn't. It also gives the platform team a cleaner migration story for later without forcing unlike process types into the same first answer.
options.explanation: This is a reasonable middle ground but it's too vague to resolve the standoff. 'Standards for monitoring and deployment while allowing variation' is exactly the kind of answer that results in years of committee work and no actual standardisation. You need to commit to concrete categories: what goes on Logic Apps and what gets a carve-out and why. The platform team and Head of Operations will interpret 'varies by category' as leaving the door open for every team to opt out whenever they feel like it.

options.text: The operational concern is valid, but I think the answer isn't forcing a single tool — it's investing in platform engineering capability so we can run diverse tools well. What we're really missing is consistent observability across workflow engines, standardised runbooks for incident response, and a common deployment pipeline that works regardless of which orchestration tool a team uses. If we invest in those cross-cutting capabilities — unified monitoring through Application Insights, standardised alerting thresholds, common CI/CD templates — we get the operational consistency the Head of Operations wants without constraining teams to a single tool that may not fit every use case. It also gives the platform team a more visible standardisation story for leadership, which is part of why this route sounds so attractive.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. You're proposing more investment as the solution to a coordination problem, which will not land well. The CTO and Head of Operations are trying to reduce complexity, not fund a platform engineering uplift. This response sounds like you're protecting your team's autonomy at the cost of organisational simplicity. Even if the argument has merit, it's the wrong answer to the specific concern about support fragmentation being raised in this room.

questions.prompt: The CTO agrees to the payroll carve-out. The Platform Architect then says: "Good. Now the compliance case management system — right now each regional team has built their own. I want to replace all of them with one Logic Apps workflow that handles the entire investigation lifecycle." The compliance team lead, who is on the call, looks uncomfortable. How should you respond?

options.text: Case management is a different class of problem from either integration workflows or domain orchestration. A compliance investigation isn't a linear process — it branches, loops, gets reassigned, goes dormant for weeks, and has complex state transitions driven by human decisions. Logic Apps can technically model this, but it will end up with hundreds of conditions in a visual designer that nobody can read or maintain. Before we commit to this, I'd want to understand whether the regional variation in how they run investigations is a problem to solve or a legitimate difference in local compliance requirements. That keeps workflow selection tied to process type instead of forcing one engine across unlike problems because standardisation sounds neat.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. You've correctly identified compliance case management as the third distinct category — long-running, human-driven, non-linear workflows with complex state. This is where Logic Apps starts to break down, not because it can't do it, but because the maintenance cost of a 500-action visual workflow with dozens of branch conditions is very high. More importantly, you've asked the right tactical question: is the regional variation a bug or a feature? If the regional teams have different regulatory requirements, forcing them into one shared workflow may be technically possible but operationally dangerous.

options.text: Replacing regional case management systems with a single centralised workflow is a significant undertaking that we need to scope carefully. We should start by understanding the data migration requirements for in-flight cases, the change management implications for regional compliance teams who've built their processes around the current tools, and the business continuity risk of migrating active investigations. I'd suggest we commission a short discovery phase — two or three weeks — to document the current state across all regions, identify the common process steps versus regional variations, and produce a migration risk assessment before we commit to a platform decision.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. You've focused on delivery risk rather than the architectural question. While migration and change management are real concerns, you've missed the more fundamental issue: whether Logic Apps is the right tool for long-running, human-driven case management at all. Listing project risks without questioning the approach will not help the compliance team lead who is clearly uncomfortable.

options.text: I support consolidating the case management systems — having five different implementations of the same investigation process is clearly inefficient and creates inconsistency in how we handle compliance cases across regions. Logic Apps with a shared data model and a common set of workflow stages — intake, triage, investigation, resolution, closure — should be able to handle this. We can parameterise the regional differences as configuration rather than separate workflows, and use a shared reporting layer so the compliance leadership gets a consistent view across all regions. The regional teams would need training on the new process, but the standardisation benefits are significant. It also gives the platform team a more visible standardisation story for leadership, which is part of why this route sounds so attractive.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. You've agreed without questioning whether the regional variation is intentional or whether Logic Apps is actually suited to this complexity. Compliance investigations in different regions may operate under different regulatory frameworks — the 'inefficiency' may be a legal obligation, not a technical failure. Treating all variation as waste before you understand the domain is exactly the kind of architectural overreach this course exists to help you avoid.

questions.prompt: After further discussion, the CTO decides compliance case management will be evaluated separately. The Platform Architect closes with: "Last item — all new workflow development must use Logic Apps from this point forward until further notice. Teams can't adopt new tools without platform review." The CTO is about to agree. How should you respond?

options.text: I can support that with one addition: the platform review process needs a clear timebox and documented criteria. If it takes six weeks to get approval for a tool choice, teams will find workarounds. Fast review for straightforward cases, escalation path for edge cases. If we agree that, I'll enforce the default-to-Logic-Apps rule in my domain. That keeps the tool choice tied to process shape, approval semantics, and failure handling instead of forcing every workflow into the same platform story for consistency alone. It also helps the room see which failure handling, state, and approval needs are genuinely different before any engine becomes the default answer.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. This is exactly the right move at this stage. You've agreed to the policy — which is reasonable — while adding the one condition that prevents it from becoming a bottleneck or causing shadow IT. A governance policy without a timebox and clear criteria always leads to either long delays or workarounds. By making your agreement conditional on a functioning review process, you've supported the standardisation goal without setting your team up for future friction.

options.text: I agree with the default-Logic-Apps principle — it gives us the standardisation the platform team and Operations are asking for. Let's document the exceptions we've agreed today — Durable Functions for payroll run orchestration and the case management evaluation as a separate workstream — and treat everything else as Logic Apps unless explicitly escalated through the platform team. We should capture this in an Architecture Decision Record so it's visible and referenceable. That way new teams joining the organisation know the default, and existing teams have clarity on what's already been decided.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This is mostly right but you've missed the crucial issue: no timebox or criteria for the review process. Agreeing to a governance gate without specifying how fast it operates or what criteria it uses is the most common cause of governance failure. You'll end up with a platform review committee that meets quarterly and blocks every exception for six months. The answer is almost right — it just needs the process guardrail.

options.text: I'm not comfortable with that level of mandatory restriction. Platform mandates that remove tool choice tend to slow teams down, create friction in the planning process, and remove the architectural flexibility we sometimes genuinely need to deliver well. I'd prefer we frame Logic Apps as the strongly recommended default with clear guidance on when it's the right fit, but leave the gate as an advisory review rather than a hard block. Teams that want to diverge present their case to the platform review, but they're not blocked from starting work while the review happens. That way we get the standardisation pressure without creating a bottleneck. It also gives the platform team a more visible standardisation story to tell leadership, which is part of why the broader path can sound reassuring in the moment.
options.explanation: You've walked away from a reasonable conclusion that the room earned through a good discussion. You agreed to carve-outs for specific cases — which means Logic Apps as the default is a sensible conclusion. Retreating to 'recommended not mandatory' at the last moment undermines the collaborative progress made during the session and makes you look like you were protecting team autonomy all along rather than engaging honestly with the coordination problem.

questions.prompt: At the end of the meeting, the CTO asks: "Can someone pull together a decision record for what we agreed? I want it before the next planning cycle." Everyone looks at the Platform Architect, who shrugs and says: "I'll cover the Logic Apps mandate. Architecture team, can you cover the exceptions and criteria?" Then they drop off the call. Everyone is now looking at you. How should you respond?

options.text: I'll write up the full decision record. I'll document the default (Logic Apps for integration, connector, and approval workflows), the agreed exceptions (Durable Functions for payroll run orchestration, case management TBD pending evaluation), and the platform review process criteria. I'll share a draft for comment by end of week and ask the platform team to review before it's finalised.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. This is the right answer. The person who doesn't own the outcome but offers to write the decision record almost always ends up with the most accurate version — because you were the one pushing for clear criteria and carve-outs throughout the meeting. By taking ownership of the full document, you ensure the nuance gets captured rather than being simplified away by the platform team's summary. End of week is fast enough to maintain momentum, and asking for platform review at the end ensures alignment without giving anyone an excuse to rewrite the conclusions.

options.text: I'll cover the exceptions and decision criteria documentation, but the Platform Architect should own the overall mandate write-up — they proposed the standardisation, they have the context on the operational requirements, and it should carry their name as the accountable party. Splitting the ownership makes sense here: they document the rule, I document the agreed exceptions and the evaluation criteria for future exceptions. We can cross-reference the two documents and both review each other's drafts to make sure we're aligned on what was agreed.
options.explanation: This is territorially reasonable but practically unhelpful. If you only write up the exceptions, the platform team's mandate document will set the frame and your exceptions will look like footnotes. Decision records work best when someone owns the whole thing. You're also more invested in the nuance being captured accurately than the platform team is — they got the broad outcome they wanted. Splitting the record is how important context gets lost.

options.text: Let me check with our engineering manager first to confirm this falls within my remit before I commit to owning the documentation. Decision records that set organisational policy typically need sign-off from the engineering leadership chain, and I want to make sure I have the authority to publish something that effectively constrains tool choices across multiple teams. I'll confirm with my manager by end of day and come back with a timeline for the write-up. I don't want to overstep my role here.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. This is exactly the wrong instinct. You've just spent a meeting exhibiting senior architectural judgement, navigating the conversation skillfully, and earning the CTO's respect on difficult calls. Asking for managerial sign-off before writing a meeting summary undoes that impression entirely. Decision records are not high-risk deliverables — writing one doesn't commit you to anything you didn't already agree to in the room. Take the action and move.
